title: Signals, Soundscapes, and Berlin in Motion
dek: A Sunday edition on what video models learn about motion, how new tools are reshaping sound design, and four Berlin dates worth putting on the calendar.

items.title: Where a video model begins to understand motion
items.summary: A mechanistic study of video encoders finds that speed and acceleration are readable early, while motion direction appears at a distinct intermediate-depth transition and is distributed across many dimensions.
items.body:
Researchers at Meta examined where physical information lives inside large video encoders using layerwise probes, subspace geometry, patch-level decoding, and targeted attention ablations. Across the architectures they tested, they found a sharp transition they call the Physics Emergence Zone. Scalar quantities such as speed and acceleration were accessible from early layers, while direction became accessible only around that transition.

The representation of direction was not a tidy, low-dimensional control. It formed a circular, high-dimensional population code that required coordinated intervention across many features. That matters for anyone treating video models as controllable motion engines: a visually simple property can be internally distributed, and the middle of the network may carry more useful physical structure than its final representation. The work was published by Meta for ICML on July 3, 2026; the underlying preprint first appeared on February 4.
items.why_selected: It is an unusually concrete look inside video models, with a finding that can change how an audiovisual practitioner thinks about motion control and model steering.

citations.title: Interpreting Physics in Video World Models
Interpreting Physics in Video World Models

items.title: Today in Berlin: seven rooms of digital work at P61
items.summary: Digital Dimensions runs at P61 Gallery from July 9, 2026 through March 13, 2027. Today, July 12, it opens at 14:00 at Potsdamer Straße 61.
items.body:
P61 Gallery's Digital Dimensions brings together technology, video art, installations, and digital experiments across seven staged rooms. The official Berlin listing describes roughly 300 works and four video shows, with contributors including BALLZS7, MARTERIUM, Philipp Tur, Vincent Schwenk, Kristian Ozer Kettner, Junanjo McLittle, Escape Routine, and Finn Moeller.

The exhibition is an immediate field trip rather than a distant save-the-date. Its listed run is July 9, 2026 through March 13, 2027, and the July 12 session begins at 14:00. The venue is P61 Gallery, Potsdamer Straße 61, 10785 Berlin. Treat the broad presentation as a scouting visit: look for one installation whose timing, spatial layout, or transition logic is worth reverse-engineering later.
items.why_selected: It is open on the edition date, directly intersects digital and audiovisual practice, and offers enough varied work to make a short research visit worthwhile.

items.title: One model for speech, effects, music, and the finished soundtrack
items.summary: Foley-Omni moves beyond isolated audio tasks by generating speech, sound effects, and music together in a shared latent process conditioned on video.
items.body:
Most video-to-audio systems solve one narrow layer at a time. Foley-Omni instead treats a complete soundtrack as a joint object, modeling speech, effects, and music within one latent generation process. Its curriculum first learns individual synthesis tasks and then extends toward full soundtrack generation, a useful design choice when the combined task is harder than any single stem.

The paper also contributes an audiovisual data curation pipeline and V2ST-Bench for evaluating holistic video soundtracks. The authors report competitive results on individual synthesis tasks and improvements in speech intelligibility, audiovisual consistency, and perceptual quality for mixed soundtrack generation. The practical signal is the shift in unit of work: rather than generate three unrelated layers and repair their collisions in the mix, train and evaluate the relationship among them from the beginning.
items.why_selected: Complete soundtrack generation is a more consequential creative target than another isolated Foley demo, and the curriculum plus benchmark make the paper inspectable.

citations.title: Foley-Omni: A Unified Multimodal Generation Model from Task-Level Audio Synthesis to Complete Video Soundtrack Generation

items.title: SoundStager turns a video narrative into an editable soundscape
items.summary: Adobe Research's CHI 2026 system proposes layered generative soundscapes that remain editable through conversational and direct controls instead of collapsing everything into one audio result.
items.body:
SoundStager starts from a familiar failure in generative audio: a creator can ask for a sound, but often cannot describe it precisely or regain control once several sounds arrive as a premixed result. The system analyzes a video's narrative and proposes layered scenes using four soundscape roles: keynote sounds, signals, soundmarks, and archetypal sounds. Creators then refine the result with both conversational and direct controls.

The interface was informed by studies with six professional sound designers and six video creators, followed by an evaluation with twelve video creators. The reported result is not simply faster generation; it is a workflow that preserves iterative control. The reusable technique is to separate narrative function from sonic material. Decide what a layer does in the scene, keep it as a stem, and only then tune its source, placement, and mix.
items.why_selected: It offers a usable vocabulary and interaction pattern for generative sound design, not only a new model or a polished demo.

citations.title: SoundStager: Interactive Design of Story-Driven GenAI Soundscapes for Video

items.title: A practical way into TouchDesigner's GPU-native POPs
items.summary: TouchDesigner 2025 moves point geometry and general numeric data into a GPU-resident operator family, opening real-time workflows for dense particles, point clouds, lighting, lasers, and data mapping.
items.body:
Point Operators, or POPs, are the first new TouchDesigner operator family in more than a decade. They create and modify 3D data on the GPU, where points can carry position, color, normals, and custom attributes. Because POPs and TOPs are both GPU based, geometry and image operations can remain resident on the device instead of repeatedly crossing to the CPU. Derivative says this supports complex animated geometry, point clouds, and particle systems with millions of points.

A sensible first study is small: create points, attach one custom attribute, visualize it, then drive color or position from a TOP without leaving the GPU. After that, inspect the included POP Guide and operator snippets. The same release extends POP data toward DMX fixtures and lasers, and adds a simulation mode to Audio Render CHOP for multiple sources, scene meshes, reflections, absorption, occlusion, and attenuation. Those connections make POPs more than a faster geometry replacement.
items.why_selected: This is a substantial production-ready change to a core audiovisual tool, and the GPU-resident mental model is immediately useful when designing a new patch.

items.title: Stockhausen's helicopter quartet becomes an immersive Berlin opera
items.summary: Mittwoch aus Licht premieres at Deutsche Oper Berlin on September 19, 2026, with further performances on September 20, 23, 26, and 27. A free helicopter-quartet livestream precedes it on September 5 at 15:00.
items.body:
Deutsche Oper Berlin is staging Karlheinz Stockhausen's Mittwoch aus Licht in the German-speaking world for the first time. Susanne Kennedy directs, Markus Selg leads stage design and video, Florent Derex handles sound design, and Maxime Pascal conducts. The production runs about four hours and combines live performance, spatial sound, video, and ritual-scale staging.

Its spectacular center is the Helicopter String Quartet: four musicians perform in four helicopters while their instrumental sound, rotor noise, and filmed images are transmitted back into the work. The opera premieres September 19 at 17:00, followed by performances September 20, 23, 26, and 27. The quartet itself flies earlier, with a free live broadcast at the Deutsche Oper on September 5 at 15:00. That broadcast is the low-commitment entry point; the full production is the deeper audiovisual study.
items.why_selected: Few events expose the engineering of space, transmission, acoustic noise, live image, and composition at this scale.

citations.title: Livestream of the Helicopter String Quartet

items.title: Better sound search starts with metadata shaped for sound designers
items.summary: AudioCards replaces thin one-sentence captions with structured acoustic attributes and sonic descriptors, improving retrieval, captioning, and metadata generation in the reported experiments.
items.body:
A sound library is only as useful as the language available to search it. AudioCards argues that ordinary captions and generic text-audio embeddings miss the structure practitioners actually use: sound class, acoustic character, sonic descriptors, and visual context. The researchers use language-model world knowledge to build structured metadata grounded in those dimensions, then train audio-language systems on it.

In their experiments, AudioCards improved text-audio retrieval, descriptive captioning, and metadata generation on professional effects libraries. It also improved general audio captioning and retrieval over a single-sentence-caption baseline. The team released a curated sound-effects dataset for further work. The immediate practice is model-independent: preserve structured fields alongside prose. A useful clip record should distinguish what produced the sound, how it behaves, what it feels like, and where it might function on screen.
items.why_selected: Metadata is unglamorous but load-bearing, and this paper supplies a concrete structure that can improve both manual libraries and future generative workflows.

citations.title: AudioCards: Structured Metadata Improves Audio Language Models For Sound Design
Audiocards: Structured Metadata Improves Audio Language Models For Sound Design

items.title: Prompt a sound by naming it, pointing at it, or marking its time
items.summary: SAM Audio unifies text, visual-mask, and temporal-span prompts in one source-separation model for speech, music, instruments, and general sound.
items.body:
Source separation normally begins with a fixed category or a single kind of prompt. SAM Audio instead lets the target be specified in three ways: describe it in text, mark the corresponding object in a video frame, or identify a time span in which it is audible. A flow-matching diffusion transformer then isolates the requested source from a mixture spanning general sounds, speech, music, and instruments.

Meta reports state-of-the-art results across its evaluation suite and introduces a real-world benchmark with human-labeled multimodal prompts plus a reference-free evaluation model. More important for creative work is the interaction pattern. A visual prompt can express what language cannot, while a short clean temporal example can disambiguate an otherwise vague sound. The released repository includes inference code and notebooks, though model checkpoints require access approval and a CUDA-capable GPU is recommended.
items.why_selected: Multimodal prompting turns source separation into a more direct editing gesture, especially when the desired sound is visible but difficult to name.

citations.title: SAM Audio: Segment Anything in Audio
SAM Audio: Segment Anything in Audio

items.title: At silent green, archives become glitches, loops, code, and sound
items.summary: The Transgressive Memory Festival takes place September 12 and 13, 2026 at silent green in Berlin, bringing media art, performance, discussion, and exhibition work to the politics of digital memory.
items.body:
Körber-Stiftung's eCommemoration Festival frames memory as something actively reconstructed by bodies, databases, networks, and algorithms. Its 2026 edition brings media art, performance, critical discussion, and an exhibition to silent green on September 12 and 13. The announced theme focuses on work that uses digital grammars such as glitches, loops, archives, image, code, and sound to unsettle linear historical narratives.

The detailed program is still to be announced, so this is a calendar hold rather than a ticket recommendation. Its value is the framing: audiovisual technique is treated as a way of organizing evidence and public memory, not only as surface. It also overlaps Berlin Art Week, making that weekend unusually dense for research across institutional, independent, and media-art contexts.
items.why_selected: The festival connects audiovisual form to archives and public memory, and its overlap with Berlin Art Week makes the weekend efficient to reserve now.

items.title: Reserve September 9 to 13 for Berlin Art Week
items.summary: Berlin Art Week runs September 9 through 13, 2026, with exhibitions, performances, film screenings, talks, gallery openings, project spaces, and collection visits across the city.
items.body:
Berlin Art Week is less one event than a five-day routing problem. The 2026 edition opens across major institutions on Wednesday, September 9. Thursday adds Positions Berlin Art Fair at Tempelhof Airport and Gallery Night. Friday shifts attention toward independent initiatives and project spaces, Saturday opens selected private collections, and Sunday closes with artist talks, guided tours, and programming at the Berlin Art Week Garten.

The full program will determine what earns time, but the dates are firm: September 9 through 13, 2026. Do not try to cover the city. Once schedules appear, choose one institutional show, one independent space, and one performance or screening with a clear audiovisual component. The Transgressive Memory Festival at silent green runs on the final weekend, providing a useful anchor for that route.
items.why_selected: It is the main citywide contemporary-art window of the autumn, and early calendar placement makes a focused route possible once the detailed program lands.
